title: Computational and physical complexity of synthesizing random multi-qudit quantum states and unitary operators
abstract: We analyze the complexity of synthesizing random states and unitary operators in a multi-qudit system in two paradigms. In one case, we consider the situation in which we manipulate the system by applying a sequence of one- and two-qudit quantum gates that constitute the elementary, and universal, gate set. The minimum number of gates required to perform the desired operation represents the computational complexity. In the other case, we consider the situation in which we manipulate the physical system using physical fields with optimized control pulses. The minimum time required to perform the desired operation represents the physical complexity. In both cases, we use analytical arguments in combination with optimal-control-theory numerical calculations to determine the complexity of random operations. We show that the computational complexity of random states or unitary operators scales exponentially with the number of qudits. Our numerical results suggest that the physical complexity of preparing random quantum states and unitary operators scales more slowly than the computational complexity. We discuss various implications of our results, especially concerning the relationship between random and pseudorandom states and unitary operators.
summary: This paper compares the gate-based computational complexity and the continuous-time physical complexity of preparing random quantum states and unitaries in multi-qudit systems. It finds that while gate requirements grow exponentially with system size, the time required for physical implementation may scale much more favorably. This distinction highlights a potential advantage in using continuous-time control for high-dimensional quantum computing.
main_problem: The paper investigates the complexity of synthesizing random multi-qudit quantum states and unitary operators using two different paradigms: computational complexity (gate count) and physical complexity (minimum operation time).
main_result: The authors show that computational complexity scales exponentially with the number of qudits, while physical complexity (continuous-time control) scales more slowly, with a conjecture that the physical complexity of pseudorandom unitaries scales at most polynomially.
method: The study combines analytical parameter-counting arguments to derive lower bounds for gate counts with numerical optimal-control-theory (specifically the GRAPE algorithm) to determine minimum synthesis times.
model_or_system: The study considers multi-qudit systems (d-level systems) under two models: a discrete gate set consisting of single- and two-qudit CZ gates, and a continuous Hamiltonian model representing superconducting qudit architectures with all-to-all inter-qudit coupling.
key_observables: Computational complexity (number of entangling gates), physical complexity (minimum operation time), and fidelity (F).
important_parameters: Number of qudits (n), dimension of the qudits (d), coupling strength (g), and the commutator order (k) in the control hierarchy.
main_assumptions: Assumes single-qudit gates are high-fidelity and easy to implement; assumes all-to-all connectivity and independent controllability of all qudit transitions; assumes the primary cost metric is the entangling gate count.
figures_summary: Table I compares theoretical lower bounds for gate counts to numerical results for various n and d; Table II displays minimum operation times for state preparation and unitary synthesis across different system sizes and dimensions.
paper_structure: The paper first defines the two complexity paradigms, then uses parameter counting to derive analytical lower bounds for gate counts, followed by numerical simulations using optimal control to evaluate physical complexity and scaling, and concludes with a discussion on the relationship between random and pseudorandom operators.
why_interesting: It provides fundamental insights into the speed limits of quantum control and the scaling laws of quantum operations, which is crucial for understanding the feasibility of implementing complex quantum algorithms in high-dimensional architectures like superconducting qudits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
